meta.title: Quiz 3 — Node.js, Express & Server-Side
meta.description: Node.js runtime, Express middleware, routing, templating, and server-side concepts.

questions.questionMd: How does the lecture describe XMLHttpRequest (XHR) compared to the Fetch API?

options.label: A
options.text: Simpler but less flexible

options.label: B
options.text: More secure and widely supported

options.label: C
options.text: Faster and more reliable

options.label: D
options.text: Clunky and harder to use

questions.explanationMd: The lecture characterizes XHR as a more awkward older API compared with Fetch.

questions.questionMd: Who created JSON according to the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Ryan Dahl

options.label: B
options.text: Douglas Crockford

options.label: C
options.text: Brendan Eich

options.label: D
options.text: Tim Berners-Lee

questions.explanationMd: Douglas Crockford popularized and specified JSON.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary purpose of the Fetch API in a web browser?

options.label: A
options.text: To serialize JavaScript objects into strings

options.label: B
options.text: To define callback functions for user interface events

options.label: C
options.text: To manipulate the DOM after a page has loaded

options.label: D
options.text: To load external resources such as text and JSON asynchronously

questions.explanationMd: Fetch is used to request resources asynchronously, such as text, JSON, or other network content.

questions.questionMd: Why is storing data as JSON preferable to storing it as plain text when using `fetch()`?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON is the only format the Fetch API is able to read

options.label: B
options.text: JSON files load faster because they are compressed by the browser

options.label: C
options.text: JSON eliminates the need to manually parse field separators when deserializing structured data

options.label: D
options.text: Plain text requires XMLHttpRequest while JSON works with fetch

questions.explanationMd: JSON can be deserialized directly into structured data, avoiding manual delimiter parsing.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following best describes the "fulfilled" state of a Promise?

options.label: A
options.text: The Promise is waiting for a `.then()` handler to be attached

options.label: B
options.text: The operation encountered a network error

options.label: C
options.text: The Promise has been created but not yet started

options.label: D
options.text: The asynchronous operation completed successfully

questions.explanationMd: A fulfilled Promise has completed successfully and produced a result.

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JavaScript object into a JSON-formatted string?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON.parse()

options.label: B
options.text: JSON.stringify()

options.label: C
options.text: JSON.encode()

options.label: D
options.text: JSON.deserialize()

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` converts a JavaScript value into a JSON string.

questions.questionMd: When chaining `.then()` calls on a fetch Promise, what does the second `.then()` receive as its argument?

options.label: A
options.text: The value returned or resolved by the first `.then()` handler

options.label: B
options.text: The HTTP status code from the first `.then()`

options.label: C
options.text: The original fetch response object

options.label: D
options.text: A new fetch Promise targeting the same resource

questions.explanationMd: Each `.then()` receives the resolved value returned from the previous step in the chain.

questions.questionMd: What does JSON stand for?

options.label: A
options.text: Java Serialized Object Network

options.label: B
options.text: JavaScript Syntax Object Notation

options.label: C
options.text: JavaScript Object Notation

options.label: D
options.text: JavaScript Ordered Node

questions.explanationMd: JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation.

questions.questionMd: Why does calling `fetch()` on a local file opened via `file://` protocol result in an error?

options.label: A
options.text: Loading files via the `file://` protocol is disallowed by the browser

options.label: B
options.text: Local files must be fetched using XMLHttpRequest instead

options.label: C
options.text: The fetch API only supports JSON files, not text files

options.label: D
options.text: JavaScript blocks all file I/O on the main thread

questions.explanationMd: Browsers restrict fetching local files directly from `file://` for security reasons.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following best describes the "pending" state of a Promise?

options.label: A
options.text: The state where `.then()` has been called but not yet attached

options.label: B
options.text: The initial state where the operation has not yet fulfilled or rejected

options.label: C
options.text: The state where the error handler has been triggered

options.label: D
options.text: The state where the operation completed but returned no data

questions.explanationMd: A Promise is pending before it settles as fulfilled or rejected.

questions.questionMd: What type of value does `JSON.stringify(object)` return?

options.label: A
options.text: A boolean indicating whether serialization succeeded

options.label: B
options.text: A Promise that resolves with the serialized data

options.label: C
options.text: A new JavaScript object with the same properties

options.label: D
options.text: A string representing the object in JSON format

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` returns a string.

questions.questionMd: How can a developer serve local files over HTTP so that `fetch()` works correctly?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the `file://` protocol with a special fetch configuration option

options.label: B
options.text: Run a local HTTP server, such as with `python -m SimpleHTTPServer`

options.label: C
options.text: Enable CORS headers directly in the HTML file

options.label: D
options.text: Switch from `fetch()` to XMLHttpRequest for local files

questions.explanationMd: Serving files over HTTP avoids the browser restrictions associated with `file://`.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, how do you attach handlers to a Promise object?

options.label: A
options.text: Via the `.then()` method

options.label: B
options.text: Via the `.attach()` method

options.label: C
options.text: By assigning them to the `onSuccess` and `onError` properties

options.label: D
options.text: By passing them as arguments directly to `fetch()`

questions.explanationMd: Handlers are attached to Promises with `.then()` and optionally `.catch()`.

questions.questionMd: In the lecture's chained fetch example, what is the correct order of asynchronous operations?

options.label: A
options.text: `fetch` completes, then `onResponse` runs, then `response.text()` completes, then `onStreamProcessed` runs

options.label: B
options.text: `onStreamProcessed` runs first, then `fetch` completes, then `onResponse` runs

options.label: C
options.text: `fetch` and `response.text()` run simultaneously before any handlers fire

options.label: D
options.text: `response.text()` runs before `fetch` completes

questions.explanationMd: The response arrives first, then the first handler runs, then the body text is resolved, then the next handler runs.

questions.questionMd: When using `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia()` with a Promise, how is the camera stream accessed?

options.label: A
options.text: By reading the return value of the function directly

options.label: B
options.text: By listening for a DOM event fired when the camera opens

options.label: C
options.text: By passing an `onSuccess` callback as the second argument

options.label: D
options.text: By attaching a `.then()` handler that receives the stream as its argument

questions.explanationMd: The Promise resolves with the media stream, which is received in a `.then()` callback.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, which statement best captures the relationship between using and defining Promise?

options.label: A
options.text: Promises are easier to define than to use

options.label: B
options.text: Promises must be fully understood internally before they can be used

options.label: C
options.text: Promises are easier to use than to define

options.label: D
options.text: Promises are equally straightforward to both define and use

questions.explanationMd: Using existing Promises is generally simpler than implementing Promise-producing logic yourself.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what is an important limitation of the `CustomEvent` API in JavaScript?

options.label: A
options.text: CustomEvents are not supported in any browser that uses the V8 engine

options.label: B
options.text: CustomEvents can only be dispatched and listened to on HTML elements, not on arbitrary class instances

options.label: C
options.text: CustomEvents can only be created inside ES6 class constructors

options.label: D
options.text: CustomEvents cannot carry any additional data in their event object

questions.explanationMd: The lecture highlights DOM-centric event dispatch/listening, not arbitrary class-instance event systems.

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JSON string back into a JavaScript object?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON.decode()

options.label: B
options.text: JSON.stringify()

options.label: C
options.text: JSON.parse()

options.label: D
options.text: JSON.hydrate()

questions.explanationMd: JSON strings are parsed into JavaScript values with `JSON.parse()`.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, which statement about `.then()` is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It can only be called once per Promise object

options.label: B
options.text: It replaces the need for the `fetch()` function

options.label: C
options.text: It immediately executes the fetch request synchronously

options.label: D
options.text: It lets you attach functions to execute when a Promise succeeds or fails

questions.explanationMd: `.then()` registers callbacks to run when a Promise settles.

questions.questionMd: What does `fetch()` return?

options.label: A
options.text: The raw text content of the requested resource

options.label: B
options.text: An XMLHttpRequest object

options.label: C
options.text: A JSON-parsed JavaScript object

options.label: D
options.text: A Promise

questions.explanationMd: `fetch()` returns a Promise.

questions.questionMd: What happens when the `await` keyword is used before a Promise?

options.label: A
options.text: It resolves instantly.

options.label: B
options.text: It throws a syntax error.

options.label: C
options.text: It pauses the execution of the async function until the Promise resolves.

options.label: D
options.text: It bypasses the `.catch` block.

questions.explanationMd: `await` is a mechanism inside `async` functions to wait for a Promise to settle (resolve or reject) before moving to the next line of code.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following is TRUE about the `status` property of a `Response` object?

options.label: A
options.text: It contains the routing configuration.

options.label: B
options.text: It contains the numeric HTTP status code (e.g., 200, 404).

options.label: C
options.text: It tells the user the state of their internet connection.

options.label: D
options.text: It logs script errors inside the browser console.

questions.explanationMd: The `Response.status` read-only property contains the status code of the response (e.g., 200 for a success).

questions.questionMd: When is the `.catch()` block executed in a Promise chain?

options.label: A
options.text: Always as the final step.

options.label: B
options.text: Only when specifically invoked.

options.label: C
options.text: When any Promise in the chain is rejected or an error is thrown.

options.label: D
options.text: If a user cancels the HTTP request.

questions.explanationMd: `.catch()` handles rejections that bubble up from anywhere in the preceding `.then()` chain.

questions.questionMd: What does the `Response.text()` method return when called inside a fetch success handler?

options.label: A
options.text: The raw bytes of the response body

options.label: B
options.text: A Promise that resolves with the response body as a string.

options.label: C
options.text: The HTML source code of the requesting page

options.label: D
options.text: The text representation of the loaded header

questions.explanationMd: Like `.json()`, `.text()` returns a Promise because parsing the body stream is an asynchronous operation.

questions.questionMd: How does the browser know what kind of data is in an HTTP response body?

options.label: A
options.text: By analyzing the body content with an algorithm

options.label: B
options.text: By checking the response body using a regular expression

options.label: C
options.text: By reading the file extension of the URL

options.label: D
options.text: By reading the `Content-Type` header in the response.

questions.explanationMd: The `Content-Type` HTTP header indicates the media type (MIME type) of the resource, such as `application/json` or `text/html`.

questions.questionMd: Which method of the Fetch API `Response` object is used to parse a JSON body?

options.label: A
options.text: .text()

options.label: B
options.text: .decode()

options.label: C
options.text: .json()

options.label: D
options.text: .parse()

questions.explanationMd: The `.json()` method of the Response interface reads the body stream and parses it as JSON.

questions.questionMd: What does `console.log()` return?

options.label: A
options.text: A string of the printed values

options.label: B
options.text: A Boolean value

options.label: C
options.text: undefined

options.label: D
options.text: The DOM node where the console output appears

questions.explanationMd: `console.log()` is a side-effect function that outputs text to the console, and inherently returns `undefined`.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, an API is a set of functions that allows what?

options.label: A
options.text: The OS to run multiple GUI apps

options.label: B
options.text: Servers to bypass security walls

options.label: C
options.text: Software applications to communicate with each other.

options.label: D
options.text: System synthesizers to access network memory

questions.explanationMd: An Application Programming Interface (API) provides a defined set of rules and protocols for building and interacting with software applications.

questions.questionMd: In the context of HTTP status codes, which of the following signifies "Not Found"?
questions.explanationMd: The HTTP 404 Not Found response code indicates that the server cannot find the requested resource.

questions.questionMd: Why are local files typically served over HTTP during development?

options.label: A
options.text: To bypass CORS restrictions that prevent local file access.

options.label: B
options.text: Because JSON parsing only works via HTTP

options.label: C
options.text: To make the application available globally on the web

options.label: D
options.text: Because the browser engine requires an active proxy

questions.explanationMd: Running a local HTTP server emulates a real web server environment, circumventing the browser's strict security protocols around `file://` URLs.
